{{Shinto Shrine | name = Kangō Shrine | location = {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}}, Kangoku-chō 6, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34|41|01|N|135|49|31.5|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Nara | Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) • Former Prefectural Shrine | consecration_year = Suiko 1 (593) | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} (three bays, cypress bark roof) | Alternate names = Manju Shrine | festival = October 17 | Rituals = April 19: Manju Matsuri
June 5: Chinhanasai
June 15: Setsuyōshu Festival
June 17: Saikusa Festival{{Cite book|lang=ja|title=Nara City History: Temples and Shrines Volume|editor=Nara City Historical Compilation Council|publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan|year=1985|page=179|isbn=4642015493}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kangou-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Main Hall (right: Nara Prefecture-designated cultural property) and Worship Hall (left) }} '''Kangō Shrine''' (漢國神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) and a former prefectural shrine under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == The shrine’s origins are traced to Suiko 1 (593), when {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi|ja|大神君白堤}} established the enshrinement of Sonokami by imperial decree.{{efn|According to the "Chronicle of the Three Deities of Ōmiwa," Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi enshrined deities at two sites: Spring Hill (Harugawa) and Sakaoka.}} Later, in Yōrō 1 (717), {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}} added two Korean deities to the main shrine. The shrine’s former names include "Sonokamikami Shrine" and "Sonokami Shrine," with "Kangami" becoming "Kangō" due to linguistic shifts. In Jōgan 1 (859), the shrine’s deities were reportedly enshrined in Kyoto’s {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内省}}, but discrepancies exist between this claim and the records of Sonokami and Kangami shrines in Kyoto. The shrine suffered destruction in 1181 during the {{ill|Burning of Nara|ja|南都焼討}} led by {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|ja|平重衡}}. It was later rebuilt in 1188 under the guidance of the high priest of Kōfuku-ji Temple. During the Keichō era (1596–1615), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} granted five tan of land in Hōren Village to support the shrine. In the Meiji period, Kangō Shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. == Enshrined Deities == * Main Deities: {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} The shrine venerates Ōmononushi as Sonokami and Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona as Karakami. According to the shrine’s legend, the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省}} in Kyoto enshrined these deities, designating them as {{ill|Myōjin-taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. == Grounds == * '''Main Hall''': A three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with cypress bark roofing, designated a Nara Prefecture tangible cultural property. * '''Rin Shrine''': Dedicated to {{ill|Hayashi Jōin|ja|林浄因}} and {{ill|Tajimamori|ja|田道間守}}, this shrine honors the "ancestor of manju" and "ancestor of confectionery." ** On April 19, the shrine holds the "Manju Festival," where sweets manufacturers offer their goods, and visitors receive free manju and tea.[http://narashikanko.or.jp/event/index.php?m=d&id=56 Manju Festival at Kangō Shrine] Nara City Tourism Association * '''Treasury''': Contains a suit of armor donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu, now a Nara City-designated cultural property. * '''Other Shrines''': Includes {{ill|Aoi Shrine|ja|葵神社}}, dedicated to Tokugawa Ieyasu; {{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nara City Board of Education informational panel == External Links == {{commonscat|Kangō Shrine}} * [http://www.kangou-jinja.jp/ Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nara shrines]] [[Category:Yamato Province Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Nara]] [[Category:Manju]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[ja:漢國神社]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[category:goodbye $1]] [[ja:林神社]] [[Category:林神社]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:漢國神社]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:images]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:漢國神社]]

{{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nara City Board of Education informational panel == External Links == {{commonscat|Kangō Shrine}} * [http://www.kangou-jinja.jp/ Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nara shrines]] [[Category:Yamato Province Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Nara]] [[Category:Manju]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Kankoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[ja:漢國神社]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Kankoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[category:goodbye $1]] [[ja:林神社]] [[Category:林神社]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:漢國神社]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:images]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[ja:漢國神社]]

{{Shinto Shrine | name = Kangō Shrine | location = {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}}, Kangoku-chō 6, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34|41|01|N|135|49|31.5|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Nara | Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) • Former Prefectural Shrine | consecration_year = Suiko 1 (593) | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} (three bays, cypress bark roof) | Alternate names = Manju Shrine | festival = October 17 | Rituals = April 19: Manju Matsuri
June 5: Chinhanasai
June 15: Setsuyōshu Festival
June 17: Saikusa Festival{{Cite book|lang=ja|title=Nara City History: Temples and Shrines Volume|editor=Nara City Historical Compilation Council|publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan|year=1985|page=179|isbn=4642015493}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kangou-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Main Hall (right: Nara Prefecture-designated cultural property) and Worship Hall (left) }} '''Kangō Shrine''' (漢國神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) and a former prefectural shrine under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == The shrine’s origins are traced to Suiko 1 (593), when {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi|ja|大神君白堤}} established the enshrinement of Sonokami by imperial decree.{{efn|According to the "Chronicle of the Three Deities of Ōmiwa," Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi enshrined deities at two sites: Spring Hill (Harugawa) and Sakaoka.}} Later, in Yōrō 1 (717), {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}} added two Korean deities to the main shrine. The shrine’s former names include "Sonokamikami Shrine" and "Sonokami Shrine," with "Kangami" becoming "Kangō" due to linguistic shifts. In Jōgan 1 (859), the shrine’s deities were reportedly enshrined in Kyoto’s {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内省}}, but discrepancies exist between this claim and the records of Sonokami and Kangami shrines in Kyoto. The shrine suffered destruction in 1181 during the {{ill|Burning of Nara|ja|南都焼討}} led by {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|ja|平重衡}}. It was later rebuilt in 1188 under the guidance of the high priest of Kōfuku-ji Temple. During the Keichō era (1596–1615), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} granted five tan of land in Hōren Village to support the shrine. In the Meiji period, Kangō Shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. == Enshrined Deities == * Main Deities: {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} The shrine venerates Ōmononushi as Sonokami and Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona as Karakami. According to the shrine’s legend, the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省}} in Kyoto enshrined these deities, designating them as {{ill|Myōjin-taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. == Grounds == * '''Main Hall''': A three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with cypress bark roofing, designated a Nara Prefecture tangible cultural property. * '''Rin Shrine''': Dedicated to {{ill|Hayashi Jōin|ja|林浄因}} and {{ill|Tajimamori|ja|田道間守}}, this shrine honors the "ancestor of manju" and "ancestor of confectionery." ** On April 19, the shrine holds the "Manju Festival," where sweets manufacturers offer their goods, and visitors receive free manju and tea.[http://narashikanko.or.jp/event/index.php?m=d&id=56 Manju Festival at Kangō Shrine] Nara City Tourism Association * '''Treasury''': Contains a suit of armor donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu, now a Nara City-designated cultural property. * '''Other Shrines''': Includes {{ill|Aoi Shrine|ja|葵神社}}, dedicated to Tokugawa Ieyasu; {{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nara City Board of Education informational panel == External Links == {{commonscat|Kangō Shrine}} * [http://www.kangou-jinja.jp/ Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nara shrines]] [[Category:Yamato Province Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Nara]] [[Category:Manju]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Kankoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[ja:漢國神社]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Kankoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[category:goodbye $1]] [[ja:林神社]] [[Category:林神社]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:漢國神社]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:images]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[ja:漢國神社]]

{{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nara City Board of Education informational panel == External Links == {{commonscat|Kangō Shrine}} * [http://www.kangou-jinja.jp/ Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nara shrines]] [[Category:Yamato Province Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Nara]] [[Category:Manju]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kankoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[ja:漢國神社]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kankoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[category:goodbye $1]] [[ja:林神社]] [[Category:林神社]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:漢國神社]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:images]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[ja:漢國神社]]

June 5: Chinhanasai
June 15: Setsuyōshu Festival
June 17: Saikusa Festival{{Cite book|lang=ja|title=Nara City History: Temples and Shrines Volume|editor=Nara City Historical Compilation Council|publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan|year=1985|page=179|isbn=4642015493}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kangou-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Main Hall (right: Nara Prefecture-designated cultural property) and Worship Hall (left) }} '''Kangō Shrine''' (漢國神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) and a former prefectural shrine under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == The shrine’s origins are traced to Suiko 1 (593), when {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi|ja|大神君白堤}} established the enshrinement of Sonokami by imperial decree.{{efn|According to the "Chronicle of the Three Deities of Ōmiwa," Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi enshrined deities at two sites: Spring Hill (Harugawa) and Sakaoka.}} Later, in Yōrō 1 (717), {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}} added two Korean deities to the main shrine. The shrine’s former names include "Sonokamikami Shrine" and "Sonokami Shrine," with "Kangami" becoming "Kangō" due to linguistic shifts. In Jōgan 1 (859), the shrine’s deities were reportedly enshrined in Kyoto’s {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内省}}, but discrepancies exist between this claim and the records of Sonokami and Kangami shrines in Kyoto. The shrine suffered destruction in 1181 during the {{ill|Burning of Nara|ja|南都焼討}} led by {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|ja|平重衡}}. It was later rebuilt in 1188 under the guidance of the high priest of Kōfuku-ji Temple. During the Keichō era (1596–1615), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} granted five tan of land in Hōren Village to support the shrine. In the Meiji period, Kangō Shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. == Enshrined Deities == * Main Deities: {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} The shrine venerates Ōmononushi as Sonokami and Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona as Karakami. {{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nara City Board of Education informational panel == External Links == {{commonscat|Kangō Shrine}} * [http://www.kangou-jinja.jp/ Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nara shrines]] [[Category:Yamato Province Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Nara]] [[Category:Manju]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kankoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[ja:漢國神社]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kankoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[category:goodbye $1]] [[ja:林神社]] [[Category:林神社]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:漢國神社]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:images]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] [[ja:漢國神社]]

{{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nara City Board of Education informational panel == External Links == {{commonscat|Kangō Shrine}} * [http://www.kangou-jinja.jp/ Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nara shrines]] [[Category:Yamato Province Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Nara]] [[Category:Manju]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kankoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[ja:漢國神社]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kankoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[category:goodbye $1]] [[ja:林神社]] [[Category:林神社]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:漢國神社]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:images]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[ja:漢國神社]]

Main Hall (right: Nara Prefecture-designated cultural property) and Worship Hall (left) }} '''Kangō Shrine''' (漢國神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) and a former prefectural shrine under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == The shrine’s origins are traced to Suiko 1 (593), when {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi|ja|大神君白堤}} established the enshrinement of Sonokami by imperial decree.{{efn|According to the "Chronicle of the Three Deities of Ōmiwa," Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi enshrined deities at two sites: Spring Hill (Harugawa) and Sakaoka.}} Later, in Yōrō 1 (717), {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}} added two Korean deities to the main shrine. The shrine’s former names include "Sonokamikami Shrine" and "Sonokami Shrine," with "Kangami" becoming "Kangō" due to linguistic shifts. In Jōgan 1 (859), the shrine’s deities were reportedly enshrined in Kyoto’s {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内省}}, but discrepancies exist between this claim and the records of Sonokami and Kangami shrines in Kyoto. The shrine suffered destruction in 1181 during the {{ill|Burning of Nara|ja|南都焼討}} led by {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|ja|平重衡}}. It was later rebuilt in 1188 under the guidance of the high priest of Kōfuku-ji Temple. During the Keichō era (1596–1615), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} granted five tan of land in Hōren Village to support the shrine. In the Meiji period, Kangō Shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. == Enshrined Deities == * Main Deities: {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} The shrine venerates Ōmononushi as Sonokami and Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona as Karakami. {{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nara City Board of Education informational panel == External Links == {{commonscat|Kangō Shrine}} * [http://www.kangou-jinja.jp/ Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nara shrines]] [[Category:Yamato Province Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Nara]] [[Category:Manju]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kankoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[ja:漢國神社]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kankoku Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]][[category:goodbye $1]] [[ja:林神社]] [[Category:林神社]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:漢國神社]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:images]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Kankoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kankoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kankoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kankoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:漢國神社]] [[Category:ppp]]

It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) and a former prefectural shrine under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == The shrine’s origins are traced to Suiko 1 (593), when {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi|ja|大神君白堤}} established the enshrinement of Sonokami by imperial decree.{{efn|According to the "Chronicle of the Three Deities of Ōmiwa," Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi enshrined deities at two sites: Spring Hill (Harugawa) and Sakaoka.}} Later, in Yōrō 1 (717), {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}} added two Korean deities to the main shrine. The shrine’s former names include "Sonokamikami Shrine" and "Sonokami Shrine," with "Kangami" becoming "Kangō" due to linguistic shifts. In Jōgan 1 (859), the shrine’s deities were reportedly enshrined in Kyoto’s {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内省}}, but discrepancies exist between this claim and the records of Sonokami and Kangami shrines in Kyoto. The shrine suffered destruction in 1181 during the {{ill|Burning of Nara|ja|南都焼討}} led by {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|ja|平重衡}}. It was later rebuilt in 1188 under the guidance of the high priest of Kōfuku-ji Temple. During the Keichō era (1596–1615), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} granted five tan of land in Hōren Village to support the shrine. The shrine’s former names include "Sonokamikami Shrine" and "Sonokami Shrine," with "Kangami" becoming "Kangō" due to linguistic shifts. In Jōgan 1 (859), the shrine’s deities were reportedly enshrined in Kyoto’s {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内省}}, but discrepancies exist between this claim and the records of Sonokami and Kangami shrines in Kyoto. The shrine suffered destruction in 1181 during the {{ill|Burning of Nara|ja|南都焼討}} led by {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|ja|平重衡}}. It was later rebuilt in 1188 under the guidance of the high priest of Kōfuku-ji Temple. During the Keichō era (1596–1615), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} granted five tan of land in Hōren Village to support the shrine. In the Meiji period, Kangō Shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. == Enshrined Deities == * Main Deities: {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} The shrine venerates Ōmononushi as Sonokami and Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona as Karakami. According to the shrine’s legend, the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省}} in Kyoto enshrined these deities, designating them as {{ill|Myōjin-taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. == Grounds == * '''Main Hall''': A three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with cypress bark roofing, designated a Nara Prefecture tangible cultural property. * '''Rin Shrine''': Dedicated to {{ill|Hayashi Jōin|ja|林浄因}} and {{ill|Tajimamori|ja|田道間守}}, this shrine honors the "ancestor of manju" and "ancestor of confectionery." ** On April 19, the shrine holds the "Manju Festival," where sweets manufacturers offer their goods, and visitors receive free manju and tea.[http://narashikanko.or.jp/event/index.php?m=d&id=56 Manju Festival at Kangō Shrine] Nara City Tourism Association * '''Treasury''': Contains a suit of armor donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu, now a Nara City-designated cultural property. * '''Other Shrines''': Includes {{ill|Aoi Shrine|ja|葵神社}}, dedicated to Tokugawa Ieyasu; {{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == {{Shinto Shrine | name = Kangō Shrine | location = {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}}, Kangoku-chō 6, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34|41|01|N|135|49|31.5|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Nara | Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) • Former Prefectural Shrine | consecration_year = Suiko 1 (593) | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} (three bays, cypress bark roof) | Alternate names = Manju Shrine | festival = October 17 | Rituals = April 19: Manju Matsuri
June 5: Chinhanasai
June 15: Setsuyōshu Festival
June 17: Saikusa Festival{{Cite book|lang=ja|title=Nara City History: Temples and Shrines Volume|editor=Nara City Historical Compilation Council|publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan|year=1985|page=179|isbn=4642015493}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kangou-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Main Hall (right: Nara Prefecture-designated cultural property) and Worship Hall (left) }} '''Kangō Shrine''' (漢國神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) and a former prefectural shrine under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == The shrine’s origins are traced to Suiko 1 (593), when {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi|ja|大神君白堤}} established the enshrinement of Sonokami by imperial decree.{{efn|According to the "Chronicle of the Three Deities of Ōmiwa," Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi enshrined deities at two sites: Spring Hill (Harugawa) and Sakaoka.}} Later, in Yōrō 1 (717), {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}} added two Korean deities to the main shrine. The shrine’s former names include "Sonokamikami Shrine" and "Sonokami Shrine," with "Kangami" becoming "Kangō" due to linguistic shifts. In Jōgan 1 (859), the shrine’s deities were reportedly enshrined in Kyoto’s {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内省}}, but discrepancies exist between this claim and the records of Sonokami and Kangami shrines in Kyoto. The shrine suffered destruction in 1181 during the {{ill|Burning of Nara|ja|南都焼討}} led by {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|ja|平重衡}}. It was later rebuilt in 1188 under the guidance of the high priest of Kōfuku-ji Temple. During the Keichō era (1596–1615), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} granted five tan of land in Hōren Village to support the shrine. In the Meiji period, Kangō Shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. == Enshrined Deities == * Main Deities: {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} The shrine venerates Ōmononushi as Sonokami and Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona as Karakami. According to the shrine’s legend, the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省}} in Kyoto enshrined these deities, designating them as {{ill|Myōjin-taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. == Grounds == * '''Main Hall''': A three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with cypress bark roofing, designated a Nara Prefecture tangible cultural property. * '''Rin Shrine''': Dedicated to {{ill|Hayashi Jōin|ja|林浄因}} and {{ill|Tajimamori|ja|田道間守}}, this shrine honors the "ancestor of manju" and "ancestor of confectionery." ** On April 19, the shrine holds the "Manju Festival," where sweets manufacturers offer their goods, and visitors receive free manju and tea.[http://narashikanko.or.jp/event/index.php?m=d&id=56 Manju Festival at Kangō Shrine] Nara City Tourism Association * '''Treasury''': Contains a suit of armor donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu, now a Nara City-designated cultural property. * '''Other Shrines''': Includes {{ill|Aoi Shrine|ja|葵神社}}, dedicated to Tokugawa Ieyasu; {{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nara City Board of Education informational panel == External Links == {{commonscat|Kangō Shrine}} * 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == {{Shinto Shrine | name = Kangō Shrine | location = {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}}, Kangoku-chō 6, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34|41|01|N|135|49|31.5|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Nara | Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) • Former Prefectural Shrine | consecration_year = Suiko 1 (593) | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} (three bays, cypress bark roof) | Alternate names = Manju Shrine | festival = October 17 | Rituals = April 19: Manju Matsuri
June 5: Chinhanasai
June 15: Setsuyōshu Festival
June 17: Saikusa Festival{{Cite book|lang=ja|title=Nara City History: Temples and Shrines Volume|editor=Nara City Historical Compilation Council|publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan|year=1985|page=179|isbn=4642015493}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kangou-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Main Hall (right: Nara Prefecture-designated cultural property) and Worship Hall (left) }} '''Kangō Shrine''' (漢國神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) and a former prefectural shrine under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == The shrine’s origins are traced to Suiko 1 (593), when {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi|ja|大神君白堤}} established the enshrinement of Sonokami by imperial decree.{{efn|According to the "Chronicle of the Three Deities of Ōmiwa," Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi enshrined deities at two sites: Spring Hill (Harugawa) and Sakaoka.}} Later, in Yōrō 1 (717), {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}} added two Korean deities to the main shrine. The shrine’s former names include "Sonokamikami Shrine" and "Sonokami Shrine," with "Kangami" becoming "Kangō" due to linguistic shifts. In Jōgan 1 (859), the shrine’s deities were reportedly enshrined in Kyoto’s {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内省}}, but discrepancies exist between this claim and the records of Sonokami and Kangami shrines in Kyoto. The shrine suffered destruction in 1181 during the {{ill|Burning of Nara|ja|南都焼討}} led by {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|ja|平重衡}}. It was later rebuilt in 1188 under the guidance of the high priest of Kōfuku-ji Temple. During the Keichō era (1596–1615), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} granted five tan of land in Hōren Village to support the shrine. In the Meiji period, Kangō Shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. == Enshrined Deities == * Main Deities: {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} The shrine venerates Ōmononushi as Sonokami and Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona as Karakami. According to the shrine’s legend, the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省}} in Kyoto enshrined these deities, designating them as {{ill|Myōjin-taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. == Grounds == * '''Main Hall''': A three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with cypress bark roofing, designated a Nara Prefecture tangible cultural property. * '''Rin Shrine''': Dedicated to {{ill|Hayashi Jōin|ja|林浄因}} and {{ill|Tajimamori|ja|田道間守}}, this shrine honors the "ancestor of manju" and "ancestor of confectionery." ** On April 19, the shrine holds the "Manju Festival," where sweets manufacturers offer their goods, and visitors receive free manju and tea.[http://narashikanko.or.jp/event/index.php?m=d&id=56 Manju Festival at Kangō Shrine] Nara City Tourism Association * '''Treasury''': Contains a suit of armor donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu, now a Nara City-designated cultural property. * '''Other Shrines''': Includes {{ill|Aoi Shrine|ja|葵神社}}, dedicated to Tokugawa Ieyasu; {{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nara City Board of Education informational panel == External Links == {{commonscat|Kangō Shrine}} * The shrine’s former names include "Sonokamikami Shrine" and "Sonokami Shrine," with "Kangami" becoming "Kangō" due to linguistic shifts. In Jōgan 1 (859), the shrine’s deities were reportedly enshrined in Kyoto’s {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内省}}, but discrepancies exist between this claim and the records of Sonokami and Kangami shrines in Kyoto. The shrine suffered destruction in 1181 during the {{ill|Burning of Nara|ja|南都焼討}} led by {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|ja|平重衡}}. It was later rebuilt in 1188 under the guidance of the high priest of Kōfuku-ji Temple. During the Keichō era (1596–1615), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} granted five tan of land in Hōren Village to support the shrine. In the Meiji period, Kangō Shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. == Enshrined Deities == * Main Deities: {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} The shrine venerates Ōmononushi as Sonokami and Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona as Karakami. According to the shrine’s legend, the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省}} in Kyoto enshrined these deities, designating them as {{ill|Myōjin-taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. == Grounds == * '''Main Hall''': A three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with cypress bark roofing, designated a Nara Prefecture tangible cultural property. * '''Rin Shrine''': Dedicated to {{ill|Hayashi Jōin|ja|林浄因}} and {{ill|Tajimamori|ja|田道間守}}, this shrine honors the "ancestor of manju" and "ancestor of confectionery." ** On April 19, the shrine holds the "Manju Festival," where sweets manufacturers offer their goods, and visitors receive free manju and tea.[http://narashikanko.or.jp/event/index.php?m=d&id=56 Manju Festival at Kangō Shrine] Nara City Tourism Association * '''Treasury''': Contains a suit of armor donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu, now a Nara City-designated cultural property. * '''Other Shrines''': Includes {{ill|Aoi Shrine|ja|葵神社}}, dedicated to Tokugawa Ieyasu; {{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == {{Shinto Shrine | name = Kangō Shrine | location = {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}}, Kangoku-chō 6, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34|41|01|N|135|49|31.5|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Nara | Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) • Former Prefectural Shrine | consecration_year = Suiko 1 (593) | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} (three bays, cypress bark roof) | Alternate names = Manju Shrine | festival = October 17 | Rituals = April 19: Manju Matsuri
June 5: Chinhanasai
June 15: Setsuyōshu Festival
June 17: Saikusa Festival{{Cite book|lang=ja|title=Nara City History: Temples and Shrines Volume|editor=Nara City Historical Compilation Council|publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan|year=1985|page=179|isbn=4642015493}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kangou-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Main Hall (right: Nara Prefecture-designated cultural property) and Worship Hall (left) }} '''Kangō Shrine''' (漢國神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) and a former prefectural shrine under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == The shrine’s origins are traced to Suiko 1 (593), when {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi|ja|大神君白堤}} established the enshrinement of Sonokami by imperial decree.{{efn|According to the "Chronicle of the Three Deities of Ōmiwa," Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi enshrined deities at two sites: Spring Hill (Harugawa) and Sakaoka.}} Later, in Yōrō 1 (717), {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}} added two Korean deities to the main shrine. The shrine’s former names include "Sonokamikami Shrine" and "Sonokami Shrine," with "Kangami" becoming "Kangō" due to linguistic shifts. In Jōgan 1 (859), the shrine’s deities were reportedly enshrined in Kyoto’s {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内省}}, but discrepancies exist between this claim and the records of Sonokami and Kangami shrines in Kyoto. The shrine suffered destruction in 1181 during the {{ill|Burning of Nara|ja|南都焼討}} led by {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|ja|平重衡}}. It was later rebuilt in 1188 under the guidance of the high priest of Kōfuku-ji Temple. During the Keichō era (1596–1615), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} granted five tan of land in Hōren Village to support the shrine. In the Meiji period, Kangō Shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. == Enshrined Deities == * Main Deities: {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} The shrine venerates Ōmononushi as Sonokami and Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona as Karakami. According to the shrine’s legend, the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省}} in Kyoto enshrined these deities, designating them as {{ill|Myōjin-taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. == Grounds == * '''Main Hall''': A three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with cypress bark roofing, designated a Nara Prefecture tangible cultural property. * '''Rin Shrine''': Dedicated to {{ill|Hayashi Jōin|ja|林浄因}} and {{ill|Tajimamori|ja|田道間守}}, this shrine honors the "ancestor of manju" and "ancestor of confectionery." ** On April 19, the shrine holds the "Manju Festival," where sweets manufacturers offer their goods, and visitors receive free manju and tea.[http://narashikanko.or.jp/event/index.php?m=d&id=56 Manju Festival at Kangō Shrine] Nara City Tourism Association * '''Treasury''': Contains a suit of armor donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu, now a Nara City-designated cultural property. * '''Other Shrines''': Includes {{ill|Aoi Shrine|ja|葵神社}}, dedicated to Tokugawa Ieyasu; {{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nara City Board of Education informational panel == External Links == {{commonscat|Kangō Shrine}} * {{Shinto Shrine | name = Kangō Shrine | location = {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}}, Kangoku-chō 6, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34|41|01|N|135|49|31.5|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Nara | Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) • Former Prefectural Shrine | consecration_year = Suiko 1 (593) | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} (three bays, cypress bark roof) | Alternate names = Manju Shrine | festival = October 17 | Rituals = April 19: Manju Matsuri
June 5: Chinhanasai
June 15: Setsuyōshu Festival
June 17: Saikusa Festival{{Cite book|lang=ja|title=Nara City History: Temples and Shrines Volume|editor=Nara City Historical Compilation Council|publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan|year=1985|page=179|isbn=4642015493}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kangou-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Main Hall (right: Nara Prefecture-designated cultural property) and Worship Hall (left) }} '''Kangō Shrine''' (漢國神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) and a former prefectural shrine under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == The shrine’s origins are traced to Suiko 1 (593), when {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi|ja|大神君白堤}} established the enshrinement of Sonokami by imperial decree.{{efn|According to the "Chronicle of the Three Deities of Ōmiwa," Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi enshrined deities at two sites: Spring Hill (Harugawa) and Sakaoka.}} Later, in Yōrō 1 (717), {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}} added two Korean deities to the main shrine. The shrine’s former names include "Sonokamikami Shrine" and "Sonokami Shrine," with "Kangami" becoming "Kangō" due to linguistic shifts. In Jōgan 1 (859), the shrine’s deities were reportedly enshrined in Kyoto’s {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内省}}, but discrepancies exist between this claim and the records of Sonokami and Kangami shrines in Kyoto. The shrine suffered destruction in 1181 during the {{ill|Burning of Nara|ja|南都焼討}} led by {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|ja|平重衡}}. It was later rebuilt in 1188 under the guidance of the high priest of Kōfuku-ji Temple. During the Keichō era (1596–1615), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} granted five tan of land in Hōren Village to support the shrine. In the Meiji period, Kangō Shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. == Enshrined Deities == * Main Deities: {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} The shrine venerates Ōmononushi as Sonokami and Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona as Karakami. According to the shrine’s legend, the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省}} in Kyoto enshrined these deities, designating them as {{ill|Myōjin-taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. == Grounds == * '''Main Hall''': A three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with cypress bark roofing, designated a Nara Prefecture tangible cultural property. * '''Rin Shrine''': Dedicated to {{ill|Hayashi Jōin|ja|林浄因}} and {{ill|Tajimamori|ja|田道間守}}, this shrine honors the "ancestor of manju" and "ancestor of confectionery." ** On April 19, the shrine holds the "Manju Festival," where sweets manufacturers offer their goods, and visitors receive free manju and tea.[http://narashikanko.or.jp/event/index.php?m=d&id=56 Manju Festival at Kangō Shrine] Nara City Tourism Association * '''Treasury''': Contains a suit of armor donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu, now a Nara City-designated cultural property. * '''Other Shrines''': Includes {{ill|Aoi Shrine|ja|葵神社}}, dedicated to Tokugawa Ieyasu; {{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == Main Hall (right: Nara Prefecture-designated cultural property) and Worship Hall (left) }} '''Kangō Shrine''' (漢國神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) and a former prefectural shrine under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == The shrine’s origins are traced to Suiko 1 (593), when {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi|ja|大神君白堤}} established the enshrinement of Sonokami by imperial decree.{{efn|According to the "Chronicle of the Three Deities of Ōmiwa," Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi enshrined deities at two sites: Spring Hill (Harugawa) and Sakaoka.}} Later, in Yōrō 1 (717), {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}} added two Korean deities to the main shrine. The shrine’s former names include "Sonokamikami Shrine" and "Sonokami Shrine," with "Kangami" becoming "Kangō" due to linguistic shifts. In Jōgan 1 (859), the shrine’s deities were reportedly enshrined in Kyoto’s {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内省}}, but discrepancies exist between this claim and the records of Sonokami and Kangami shrines in Kyoto. The shrine suffered destruction in qq81 during the {{ill|Burning of Nara|ja|南都焼討}} led by {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|ja|平重衡}}. It was later rebuilt in qq88 under the guidance of the high priest of Kōfuku-ji Temple. During the Keichō era (1596–1615), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} granted five tan of land in Hōren Village to support the shrine. In the Meiji period, Kangō Shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. == Enshrined Deities == * Main Deities: {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} The shrine venerates Ōmononushi as Sonokami and Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona as Karakami. According to the shrine’s legend, the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省}} in Kyoto enshrined these deities, designating them as {{ill|Myōjin-taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. == Jōin|ja|林浄因}} and {{ill|Tajimamori|ja|田道間守}}, this shrine honors the "ancestor of manju" and "ancestor of confectionery." ** On April 19, the shrine holds the "Manju Festival," where sweets manufacturers offer their goods, and visitors receive free manju and tea.[http://narashikanko.or.jp/event/index.php?m=d&id=56 Manju Festival at Kangō Shrine] Nara City Tourism Association * '''Treasury''': Contains a suit of armor donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu, now a Nara City-designated cultural property. * '''Other Shrines''': Includes {{ill|Aoi Shrine|ja|葵神社}}, dedicated to Tokugawa Ieyasu; {{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in The shrine’s former names include "Sonokamikami Shrine" and "Sonokami Shrine," with "Kangami" becoming "Kangō" due to linguistic shifts. In Jōgan 1 (859), the shrine’s deities were reportedly enshrined in Kyoto’s {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内省}}, but discrepancies exist between this claim and the records of Sonokami and Kangami shrines in Kyoto. The shrine suffered destruction in qq81 during the {{ill|Burning of Nara|ja|南都焼討}} led by {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|ja|平重衡}}. It was later rebuilt in qq88 under the guidance of the high priest of Kōfuku-ji Temple. During the Keichō era (1596–1615), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} granted five tan of land in Hōren Village to support the shrine. In the Meiji period, Kangō Shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. == Enshrined Deities == * Main Deities: {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} The shrine venerates Ōmononushi as Sonokami and Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona as Karakami. According to the shrine’s legend, the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省}} in Kyoto enshrined these deities, designating them as {{ill|Myōjin-taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. == Grounds == * '''Main Hall''': A three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with cypress bark roofing, designated a Nara Prefecture tangible cultural property. * '''Rin Shrine''': Dedicated to {{ill|Hayashi Jōin|ja|林浄因}} and {{ill|Tajimamori|ja|田道間守}}, this shrine honors the "ancestor of manju" and "ancestor of confectionery." ** On April 19, the shrine holds the "Manju Festival," where sweets manufacturers offer their goods, and visitors receive free manju and tea.[http://narashikanko.or.jp/event/index.php?m=d&id=56 Manju Festival at Kangō Shrine] Nara City Tourism Association * '''Treasury''': Contains a suit of armor donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu, now a Nara City-designated cultural property. * '''Other Shrines''': Includes {{ill|Aoi Shrine|ja|葵神社}}, dedicated to Tokugawa Ieyasu; {{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅}}: qq minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == {{Shinto Shrine | name = Kangō Shrine | location = {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}}, Kangoku-chō 6, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34|41|01|N|135|49|31.5|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Nara | Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) • Former Prefectural Shrine | consecration_year = Suiko 1 (593) | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} (three bays, cypress bark roof) | Alternate names = Manju Shrine | festival = October 17 | Rituals = April 19: Manju Matsuri
June 5: Chinhanasai
June 15: Setsuyōshu Festival
June 17: Saikusa Festival{{Cite book|lang=ja|title=Nara City History: Temples and Shrines Volume|editor=Nara City Historical Compilation Council|publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan|year=1985|page=179|isbn=4642015493}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kangou-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Main Hall (right: Nara Prefecture-designated cultural property) and Worship Hall (left) }} '''Kangō Shrine''' (漢國神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) and a former prefectural shrine under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == The shrine’s origins are traced to Suiko 1 (593), when {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi|ja|大神君白堤}} established the enshrinement of Sonokami by imperial decree.{{efn|According to the "Chronicle of the Three Deities of Ōmiwa," Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi enshrined deities at two sites: Spring Hill (Harugawa) and Sakaoka.}} Later, in Yōrō 1 (717), {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}} added two Korean deities to the main shrine. The shrine’s former names include "Sonokamikami Shrine" and "Sonokami Shrine," with "Kangami" becoming "Kangō" due to linguistic shifts. In Jōgan 1 (859), the shrine’s deities were reportedly enshrined in Kyoto’s {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内省}}, but discrepancies exist between this claim and the records of Sonokami and Kangami shrines in Kyoto. The shrine suffered destruction in 1181 during the {{ill|Burning of Nara|ja|南都焼討}} led by {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|ja|平重衡}}. It was later rebuilt in 1188 under the guidance of the high priest of Kōfuku-ji Temple. During the Keichō era (1596–1615), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} granted five tan of land in Hōren Village to support the shrine. In the Meiji period, Kangō Shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. == Enshrined Deities == * Main Deities: {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} The shrine venerates Ōmononushi as Sonokami and Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona as Karakami. According to the shrine’s legend, the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省}} in Kyoto enshrined these deities, designating them as {{ill|Myōjin-taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. == Grounds == * '''Main Hall''': A three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with cypress bark roofing, designated a Nara Prefecture tangible cultural property. * '''Rin Shrine''': Dedicated to {{ill|Hayashi Jōin|ja|林浄因}} and {{ill|Tajimamori|ja|田道間守}}, this shrine honors the "ancestor of manju" and "ancestor of confectionery." ** On April 19, the shrine holds the "Manju Festival," where sweets manufacturers offer their goods, and visitors receive free manju and tea.[http://narashikanko.or.jp/event/index.php?m=d&id=56 Manju Festival at Kangō Shrine] Nara City Tourism Association * '''Treasury''': Contains a suit of armor donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu, now a Nara City-designated cultural property. * '''Other Shrines''': Includes {{ill|Aoi Shrine|ja|葵神社}}, dedicated to Tokugawa Ieyasu; {{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nara City Board of Education informational panel == External Links == {{commonscat|Kangō Shrine}} * June 5: Chinhanasai
June 15: Setsuyōshu Festival
June 17: Saikusa Festival{{Cite book|lang=ja|title=Nara City History: Temples and Shrines Volume|editor=Nara City Historical Compilation Council|publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan|year=1985|page=179|isbn=4642015493}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kangou-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Main Hall (right: Nara Prefecture-designated cultural property) and Worship Hall (left) }} '''Kangō Shrine''' (漢國神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) and a former prefectural shrine under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == The shrine’s origins are traced to Suiko 1 (593), when {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi|ja|大神君白堤}} established the enshrinement of Sonokami by imperial decree.{{efn|According to the "Chronicle of the Three Deities of Ōmiwa," Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi enshrined deities at two sites: Spring Hill (Harugawa) and Sakaoka.}} Later, in Yōrō 1 (717), {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}} added two Korean deities to the main shrine. The shrine’s former names include "Sonokamikami Shrine" and "Sonokami Shrine," with "Kangami" becoming "Kangō" due to linguistic shifts. In Jōgan 1 (859), the shrine’s deities were reportedly enshrined in Kyoto’s {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内省}}, but discrepancies exist between this claim and the records of Sonokami and Kangami shrines in Kyoto. The shrine suffered destruction in 1181 during the {{ill|Burning of Nara|ja|南都焼討}} led by {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|ja|平重衡}}. It was later rebuilt in 1188 under the guidance of the high priest of Kōfuku-ji Temple. During the Keichō era (1596–1615), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} granted five tan of land in Hōren Village to support the shrine. In the Meiji period, Kangō Shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. == Enshrined Deities == * Main Deities: {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} The shrine venerates Ōmononushi as Sonokami and Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona as Karakami. According to the shrine’s legend, the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省}} in Kyoto enshrined these deities, designating them as {{ill|Myōjin-taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. == Grounds == * '''Main Hall''': A three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with cypress bark roofing, designated a Nara Prefecture tangible cultural property. * '''Rin Shrine''': Dedicated to {{ill|Hayashi Jōin|ja|林浄因}} and {{ill|Tajimamori|ja|田道間守}}, this shrine honors the "ancestor of manju" and "ancestor of confectionery." ** On April 19, the shrine holds the "Manju Festival," where sweets manufacturers offer their goods, and visitors receive free manju and tea.[http://narashikanko.or.jp/event/index.php?m=d&id=56 Manju Festival at Kangō Shrine] Nara City Tourism Association * '''Treasury''': Contains a suit of armor donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu, now a Nara City-designated cultural property. * '''Other Shrines''': Includes {{ill|Aoi Shrine|ja|葵神社}}, dedicated to Tokugawa Ieyasu; {{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == It was later rebuilt in 1188 under the guidance of the high priest of Kōfuku-ji Temple. During the Keichō era (1596–1615), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} granted five tan of land in Hōren Village to support the shrine. According to the shrine’s legend, the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省}} in Kyoto enshrined these deities, designating them as {{ill|Myōjin-taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. == Grounds == * '''Main Hall''': A three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with cypress bark roofing, designated a Nara Prefecture tangible cultural property. * '''Rin Shrine''': Dedicated to {{ill|Hayashi Jōin|ja|林浄因}} and {{ill|Tajimamori|ja|田道間守}}, this shrine honors the "ancestor of manju" and "ancestor of confectionery." ** On April 19, the shrine holds the "Manju Festival," where sweets manufacturers offer their goods, and visitors receive free manju and tea.[http://narashikanko.or.jp/event/index.php?m=d&id=56 Manju Festival at Kangō Shrine] Nara City Tourism Association * '''Treasury''': Contains a suit of armor donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu, now a Nara City-designated cultural property. * '''Other Shrines''': Includes {{ill|Aoi Shrine|ja|葵神社}}, dedicated to Tokugawa Ieyasu; {{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara The shrine’s former names include "Sonokamikami Shrine" and "Sonokami Shrine," with "Kangami" becoming "Kangō" due to linguistic shifts. In Jōgan 1 (859), the shrine’s deities were reportedly enshrined in Kyoto’s {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内省}}, but discrepancies exist between this claim and the records of Sonokami and Kangami shrines in Kyoto. It was later rebuilt in 1188 under the guidance of the high priest of Kōfuku-ji Temple. According to the shrine’s legend, the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省}} in Kyoto enshrined these deities, designating them as {{ill|Myōjin-taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. == Grounds == * '''Main Hall''': A three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with cypress bark roofing, designated a Nara Prefecture tangible cultural property. * '''Rin Shrine''': Dedicated to {{ill|Hayashi Jōin|ja|林浄因}} and {{ill|Tajimamori|ja|田道間守}}, this shrine honors the "ancestor of manju" and "ancestor of confectionery." ** On April 19, the shrine holds the "Manju Festival," where sweets manufacturers offer their goods, and visitors receive free manju and tea.[http://narashikanko.or.jp/event/index.php?m=d&id=56 Manju Festival at Kangō Shrine] Nara City Tourism Association * '''Treasury''': Contains a suit of armor donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu, now a Nara City-designated cultural property. * '''Other Shrines''': Includes {{ill|Aoi Shrine|ja|葵神社}}, dedicated to Tokugawa Ieyasu; {{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == {{Shinto Shrine | name = Kangō Shrine | location = {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}}, Kangoku-chō 6, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34|41|01|N|135|49|31.5|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Nara | Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) • Former Prefectural Shrine | consecration_year = Suiko 1 (593) | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} (three bays, cypress bark roof) | alternate_name = Manju Shrine | festival = October 17 | Rituals = April 19: Manju Matsuri
June 5: Chinhanasai
June 15: Setsuyōshu Festival
June 17: Saikusa Festival{{Cite book|lang=ja|title=Nara City History: Temples and Shrines Volume|editor=Nara City Historical Compilation Council|publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan|year=1985|page=179|isbn=4642015493}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kangou-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Main Hall (right: Nara Prefecture-designated cultural property) and Worship Hall (left) }} '''Kangō Shrine''' (漢國神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor, disputable site) and a former prefectural shrine under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == The shrine’s origins are traced to Suiko 1 (593), when {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi|ja|大神君白堤}} established the enshrinement of Sonokami by imperial decree.{{efn|According to the "Chronicle of the Three Deities of Ōmiwa," Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi enshrined deities at two sites: Spring Hill (Harugawa) and Sakaoka.}} Later, in Yōrō 1 (717), {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}} added two Korean deities to the main shrine. The shrine’s former names include "Sonokamikami Shrine" and "Sonokami Shrine," with "Kangami" becoming "Kangō" due to linguistic shifts. In Jōgan 1 (859), the shrine’s deities were reportedly enshrined in Kyoto’s {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内省}}, but discrepancies exist between this claim and the records of Sonokami and Kangami shrines in Kyoto. The shrine suffered destruction in 1181 during the {{ill|Burning of Nara|ja|南都焼討}} led by {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|ja|平重衡}}. It was later rebuilt in 1188 under the guidance of the high priest of Kōfuku-ji Temple. During the Keichō era (1596–1615), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} granted five tan of land in Hōren Village to support the shrine. In the Meiji period, Kangō Shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. == Enshrined Deities == * Main Deities: {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona}} The shrine venerates Ōmononushi as Sonokami and Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona as Karakami. According to the shrine’s legend, the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省}} in Kyoto enshrined these deities, designating them as {{ill|Myōjin-taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. == Grounds == * '''Main Hall''': A three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with cypress bark roofing, designated a Nara Prefecture tangible cultural property. * '''Rin Shrine''': Dedicated to {{ill|Hayashi Jōin|ja|林浄因}} and {{ill|Tajimamori|ja|田道間守}}, this shrine honors the "ancestor of manju" and "ancestor of confectionery." ** On April 19, the shrine holds the "Manju Festival," where sweets manufacturers offer their goods, and visitors receive free manju and tea.[http://narashikanko.or.jp/event/index.php?m=d&id=56 Manju Festival at Kangō Shrine] Nara City Tourism Association * '''Treasury''': Contains a suit of armor donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu, now a Nara City-designated cultural property. * '''Other Shrines''': Includes {{ill|Aoi Shrine|ja|葵神社}}, dedicated to Tokugawa Ieyasu; {{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nara City Board of Education informational panel == External Links == {{commonscat|Kangō Shrine}} * {{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社|12=draft}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅|12=draft}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅|12=draft}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nara City Board of Education informational panel == External Links == {{commonscat|Kangō Shrine}} * [http://www.kangou-jinja.jp/ Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Nara shrines]] [[Category:Yamato Province Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Nara]] [[Category:Manju]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:林神社]] [[Category:漢國神社]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Kankoku Shrine]] [[ja:漢國神社]] [[ja:林神社]] [[sn:ankoku Shrine]]

{{Shinto Shrine | name = Kangō Shrine | location = {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市|12=draft}}, Kangoku-chō 6, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34|41|01|N|135|49|31.5|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Nara | Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} (minor, disputable site) • Former Prefectural Shrine | consecration_year = Suiko 1 (593) | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|12=draft}} (three bays, cypress bark roof) | alternate_name = Manju Shrine | festival = October 17 | Rituals = April 19: Manju Matsuri
June 5: Chinhanasai
June 15: Setsuyōshu Festival
June 17: Saikusa Festival{{Cite book|lang=ja|title=Nara City History: Temples and Shrines Volume|editor=Nara City Historical Compilation Council|publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan|year=1985|page=179|isbn=4642015493}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kangou-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Main Hall (right: Nara Prefecture-designated cultural property) and Worship Hall (left) }} '''Kangō Shrine''' (漢國神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} (minor, disputable site) and a former prefectural shrine under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == The shrine’s origins are traced to Suiko 1 (593), when {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi|ja|大神君白堤|12=draft}} established the enshrinement of Sonokami by imperial decree.{{efn|According to the "Chronicle of the Three Deities of Ōmiwa," Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi enshrined deities at two sites: Spring Hill (Harugawa) and Sakaoka.}} Later, in Yōrō 1 (717), {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等|12=draft}} added two Korean deities to the main shrine. The shrine’s former names include "Sonokamikami Shrine" and "Sonokami Shrine," with "Kangami" becoming "Kangō" due to linguistic shifts. In Jōgan 1 (859), the shrine’s deities were reportedly enshrined in Kyoto’s {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内省|12=draft}}, but discrepancies exist between this claim and the records of Sonokami and Kangami shrines in Kyoto. The shrine suffered destruction in 1181 during the {{ill|Burning of Nara|ja|南都焼討|12=draft}} led by {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|ja|平重衡|12=draft}}. It was later rebuilt in 1188 under the guidance of the high priest of Kōfuku-ji Temple. During the Keichō era (1596–1615), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|12=draft}} granted five tan of land in Hōren Village to support the shrine. In the Meiji period, Kangō Shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. == Enshrined Deities == * Main Deities: {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft}} The shrine venerates Ōmononushi as Sonokami and Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona as Karakami. According to the shrine’s legend, the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省|12=draft}} in Kyoto enshrined these deities, designating them as {{ill|Myōjin-taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}. == Grounds == * '''Main Hall''': A three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with cypress bark roofing, designated a Nara Prefecture tangible cultural property. * '''Rin Shrine''': Dedicated to {{ill|Hayashi Jōin|ja|林浄因|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tajimamori|ja|田道間守|12=draft}}, this shrine honors the "ancestor of manju" and "ancestor of confectionery." ** On April 19, the shrine holds the "Manju Festival," where sweets manufacturers offer their goods, and visitors receive free manju and tea.[http://narashikanko.or.jp/event/index.php?m=d&id=56 Manju Festival at Kangō Shrine] Nara City Tourism Association * '''Treasury''': Contains a suit of armor donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu, now a Nara City-designated cultural property. * '''Other Shrines''': Includes {{ill|Aoi Shrine|ja|葵神社|12=draft}}, dedicated to Tokugawa Ieyasu; {{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社|12=draft}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅|12=draft}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅|12=draft}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nara City Board of Education informational panel == External Links == {{commonscat|Kangō Shrine}} * [http://www.kangou-jinja.jp/ Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Nara shrines]] [[Category:Yamato Province Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Nara]] [[Category:Manju]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:林神社]] [[Category:漢國神社]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Kankoku Shrine]] [[ja:漢國神社]] [[ja:林神社]] [[sn:ankoku Shrine]]

In the Meiji period, Kangō Shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. == Enshrined Deities == * Main Deities: {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft|12=draft}} The shrine venerates Ōmononushi as Sonokami and Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona as Karakami. According to the shrine’s legend, the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省|12=draft|12=draft}} in Kyoto enshrined these deities, designating them as {{ill|Myōjin-taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Grounds == * '''Main Hall''': A three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with cypress bark roofing, designated a Nara Prefecture tangible cultural property. * '''Rin Shrine''': Dedicated to {{ill|Hayashi Jōin|ja|林浄因|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tajimamori|ja|田道間守|12=draft|12=draft}}, this shrine honors the "ancestor of manju" and "ancestor of confectionery." ** On April 19, the shrine holds the "Manju Festival," where sweets manufacturers offer their goods, and visitors receive free manju and tea.[http://narashikanko.or.jp/event/index.php?m=d&id=56 Manju Festival at Kangō Shrine] Nara City Tourism Association * '''Treasury''': Contains a suit of armor donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu, now a Nara City-designated cultural property. * '''Other Shrines''': Includes {{ill|Aoi Shrine|ja|葵神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, dedicated to Tokugawa Ieyasu; {{ill|Gennkurou Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社|12=draft|12=draft}}; and various other subsidiary shrines. == Cultural Properties == * '''Nara Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Main Hall (designated in 1951)Nara City Board of Education informational panel. * '''Nara City Tangible Cultural Property''': ** Armor (now housed at the Nara National Museum). == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅|12=draft|12=draft}}: 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S. * {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅|12=draft|12=draft}}: 11 minutes on foot from the East Exit. * By bus: Disembark at "Kintetsu Nara Station" and walk 3 minutes. == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nara City Board of Education informational panel == External Links == {{commonscat|Kangō Shrine}} * [http://www.kangou-jinja.jp/ Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Nara shrines]] [[Category:Yamato Province Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Nara]] [[Category:Manju]] [[Category:Kankoku Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:林神社]] [[Category:漢國神社]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Kankoku Shrine]] [[ja:漢國神社]] [[ja:林神社]] [[sn:ankoku Shrine]]

Main Hall (right: Nara Prefecture-designated cultural property) and Worship Hall (left) }} '''Kangō Shrine''' (漢國神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft|12=draft}} (minor, disputable site) and a former prefectural shrine under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == The shrine’s origins are traced to Suiko 1 (593), when {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi|ja|大神君白堤|12=draft|12=draft}} established the enshrinement of Sonokami by imperial decree.{{efn|According to the "Chronicle of the Three Deities of Ōmiwa," Ōmiwa no Kimi Shiratsutsumi enshrined deities at two sites: Spring Hill (Harugawa) and Sakaoka.}} Later, in Yōrō 1 (717), {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等|12=draft|12=draft}} added two Korean deities to the main shrine. The shrine’s former names include "Sonokamikami Shrine" and "Sonokami Shrine," with "Kangami" becoming "Kangō" due to linguistic shifts. In Jōgan 1 (859), the shrine’s deities were reportedly enshrined in Kyoto’s {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|ja|宮内省|12=draft|12=draft}}, but discrepancies exist between this claim and the records of Sonokami and Kangami shrines in Kyoto. The shrine suffered destruction in 1181 during the {{ill|Burning of Nara|ja|南都焼討|12=draft|12=draft}} led by {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|ja|平重衡|12=draft|12=draft}}. It was later rebuilt in 1188 under the guidance of the high priest of Kōfuku-ji Temple. During the Keichō era (1596–1615), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|12=draft|12=draft}} granted five tan of land in Hōren Village to support the shrine. In the Meiji period, Kangō Shrine was designated a prefectural shrine. == Enshrined Deities == * Main Deities: {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft|12=draft}} The shrine venerates Ōmononushi as Sonokami and Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona as Karakami.
